---
title: "Dog Weights"
output: 
  html_document:
    code_folding: hide
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
# global default settings for chunks
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
                      fig.width = 10, 
                      fig.align = "center"
                      )
# loaded packages; placed here to be able to load global settings
Packages <- c("tidyverse", "dplyr", "rvest", "httr", "XML", "readxl", "fragilityindex", "kableExtra")
library(patchwork)
library(ggridges)
library(plotly)
library(anytime)
invisible(lapply(Packages, library, character.only = TRUE))
# global settings for color palettes
options(
  ggplot2.continuous.colour = "viridis",
  ggplot2.continuous.fill = "viridis"
)
scale_colour_discrete = scale_colour_viridis_d
scale_fill_discrete = scale_fill_viridis_d
# theme global setting for ggplot
theme_set(theme_minimal() + 
            theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
            theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
                  plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 8)))
```
<HR style="FILTER: alpha(opacity=100,finishopacity=0,style=3)" width="80%" color=#987cb9 SIZE=3>
<HR style="FILTER: alpha(opacity=100,finishopacity=0,style=1)" width="80%" color=#987cb9 SIZE=3>
### Data at the first glance

In order to get an idea of how this dataset is like, we utilize `view ()` and `skimr::skim()` function to do so. Before moving on to the next step, we find an unexpected result (in the 77th row, see below) within the dataset, so we decided to remove it.
```{r}
dogweights = read_excel("./data/dog _weights.xlsx", range = "B3:E185") 

# A tibble: 182 x 4
#  DATE  Raisin Simone Gagne
#  <chr> <chr>  <chr>  <chr>
#1 43324 19 12  10 14  10 10
#2 43331 20 03  11 02  10 12
#3 43334 20 04  11 04  10 13
#4 43336 19 13  11 04  11 01
#5 43339 19 06  10 08  10 06
#6 43340 NA     NA     NA   
#7 43341 19 06  11 00  11 01
#8 43342 NA     NA     NA   
#9 43343 19 09  11 00  10 12

# skimr::skim(dogweights)

# -- Data Summary ------------------------
#                          Values    
#Name                       dogweights
#Number of rows             182       
#Number of columns          4         
#_______________________              
#Column type frequency:               
#  character                4         
#________________________             
#Group variables            None      

#-- Variable type: character ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# A tibble: 4 x 8
#  skim_variable n_missing complete_rate   min   max empty n_unique whitespace
#* <chr>             <int>         <dbl> <int> <int> <int>    <int>      <int>
#1 DATE                  0         1         1     5     0      182          0
#2 Raisin                3         0.984     4     5     0       45          0
#3 Simone                8         0.956     4     5     0       33          0
#4 Gagne                62         0.659     4     5     0       35          0
```

```{r, echo=FALSE}
dogweights[77,]  %>%  
  knitr::kable(align = 'c', digits = 3) %>%
  kable_styling(full_width = F) %>%
column_spec(1, bold = T, color = "black") %>%
  column_spec(1, width = "10em")
```

<HR style="FILTER: alpha(opacity=100,finishopacity=0,style=1)" width="80%" color=#987cb9 SIZE=3>

### Data cleaning
At initial stage, all the variables are stored as characters, which might not be what we desired. In order to produce *tidy* dataset, we need to:

*	Convert `Date` variable to the right data class by `as.Date`. We can also get the number of days easily through this function.

* We want to show each Dogs’ weight data in pounds. Therefore, `separate` function is applied to divide Lbs and Oz. After that, we’re allowed to convert the weights by $1Lbs = 16Oz$.

Here is the head and tail of the clean data:  (Left:  Head,  Right:  Tail)
```{r}
dogweights = read_excel("./data/dog _weights.xlsx", range = "B3:E185")[-77,] %>% 
  janitor::clean_names() %>% 
  mutate(
    date = as.Date(as.numeric(date), origin = "1899-12-30")) %>%
  # raisin
  separate(raisin, into = c("raisin_lbs", "raisin_os"), sep = " ") %>% 
  mutate(raisin_lbs = round(as.numeric(raisin_lbs) + as.numeric(raisin_os)/16, digits = 2)) %>%
  # simone
  separate(simone, into = c("simone_lbs", "simone_os"), sep = " ") %>%
  mutate(simone_lbs = round(as.numeric(simone_lbs) + as.numeric(simone_os)/16, digits = 2)) %>%
  # gagne
  separate(gagne, into = c("gagne_lbs", "gagne_os"), sep = " ") %>%
  mutate(gagne_lbs = round(as.numeric(gagne_lbs) + as.numeric(gagne_os)/16, digits = 2)) %>% 
  select(date, raisin_lbs, simone_lbs, gagne_lbs)

cbind(dogweights[1:5,], dogweights[175:179,]) %>%  
  knitr::kable(align = 'c', digits = 3)%>%
  kable_styling(full_width = F) %>%
column_spec(1, bold = T, color = "black") %>%
  column_spec(2, width = "10em")
```

From the tidy dataset, we can see the owner recorded three dogs' weights from 2018-08-12 (which is my birthday!omg) to 2020-06-13, 671 days in total. 

Following up, time for some data visualizations.

<HR style="FILTER: alpha(opacity=100,finishopacity=0,style=1)" width="80%" color=#987cb9 SIZE=3>

### Visualization

Before we get started, weight data is spread across three columns, which correspond to three different puppies. To facilitate further process, we apply `pivot_longer` and change the data to long format.

```{r}
weights_time = 
  dogweights %>%
  rename(
      Raisin = raisin_lbs, 
      Simone = simone_lbs, 
      Gagne = gagne_lbs) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    Raisin:Gagne,
    names_to = "Name",
    values_to = "Weights")
```

#### *Weights of dogs*
We can get detailed information by placing cursor over the graph. (Mean, Median, Max/Min) There’s an outlier (7.94Lbs) in Raisin’s weight that doesn’t seem quite realistic, so we think of it as a typo and will remove it in following analysis. 
We can infer from the plot that Gagne and Simone might belong to a small-sized family like Pomeranians, while Rasian should belong to another larger-sized breed.
```{r}
plot_weights = 
  ggplot(weights_time, aes(x = Name, y = Weights, fill = Name)) +
  labs(title = "Weights of dogs",
       x = "Dog",
       y = "Lbs") + 
  stat_summary(fun.y = median, geom = "point", color = "blue", size = 3) + 
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.5, size = 0.5, color = "blue")

ggplotly(plot_weights)
```

<HR style="FILTER: alpha(opacity=100,finishopacity=0,style=3)" width="80%" color=#987cb9 SIZE=1>

#### *Weights over time*
*The light green shaded areas* indicate the time when these dogs are not under their owner’s care. By zooming in these areas, we can find that the **dogs’ weights continue to drop** until January 2019 because their owner was constantly boarding them with Weber or Kennel. After that, the dogs’ weights rose slowly and steadily while their owners stayed with them.  *The Light blue dashed lines* indicate dates that the dogs go on a haircut. In the enlarged plot, we can find that Raisin lost more weight after haircut than Gagne and Simone did. Therefore, we assume that Raisin is a member of the long-haired breed, while the others are not.
```{r}
plot_time = 
  ggplot(weights_time[-364,], aes(x = date, y = Weights, color = Name)) +
  labs(title = "Weights over time",
       subtitle = "Blue line: ",
       x = "Time",
       y = "Lbs") + 
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 0.7) + 
  geom_smooth(size = 0.5, alpha = .9, se = F) + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(as.numeric(as.Date(43389,  origin = "1899-12-30")), 
                            as.numeric(as.Date(43974,  origin = "1899-12-30"))), 
             linetype="dashed", color = "light blue", size=.3) + 
  annotate("text", x = as.Date(43780,  origin = "1899-12-30"), y = 8.2, label="Gagne died:(") + 
  annotate("text", x = c(as.Date(43389,  origin = "1899-12-30"), 
                         as.Date(43974,  origin = "1899-12-30")), y = 7.5, label="Hair cut") + 
  annotate("rect", xmin=as.Date(43336,  origin = "1899-12-30"), xmax=as.Date(43339,  origin = "1899-12-30"), ymin=9, ymax=20, alpha=.2,fill="light green") + 
  annotate("rect", xmin=as.Date(43359,  origin = "1899-12-30"), xmax=as.Date(43374,  origin = "1899-12-30"), ymin=9, ymax=20, alpha=.2,fill="light green") + 
  annotate("rect", xmin=as.Date(43377,  origin = "1899-12-30"), xmax=as.Date(43382,  origin = "1899-12-30"), ymin=9, ymax=20, alpha=.2,fill="light green") + 
  annotate("rect", xmin=as.Date(43410,  origin = "1899-12-30"), xmax=as.Date(43422,  origin = "1899-12-30"), ymin=9, ymax=20, alpha=.2,fill="light green") + 
  annotate("rect", xmin=as.Date(43452,  origin = "1899-12-30"), xmax=as.Date(43473,  origin = "1899-12-30"), ymin=9, ymax=20, alpha=.2,fill="light green") + 
  annotate("rect", xmin=as.Date(43760,  origin = "1899-12-30"), xmax=as.Date(43773,  origin = "1899-12-30"), ymin=9, ymax=20, alpha=.2,fill="light green") + 
  annotate("rect", xmin=as.Date(43859,  origin = "1899-12-30"), xmax=as.Date(43864,  origin = "1899-12-30"), ymin=9, ymax=20, alpha=.2,fill="light green")

ggplotly(plot_time)
```

<HR style="FILTER: alpha(opacity=100,finishopacity=0,style=1)" width="80%" color=#987cb9 SIZE=3>
### Discussion

RIP for Gagne first. We can see that in the last months of its life it's losing weight little by little:( I can't imagine how she must feel. From these results, I think we should spend as much time as possible with our lovely dogs and take care of them ourselves so that they can accompany us through a longer and happier life. Also, we can see that the weight of the dogs tended to move in the same direction (up and down), and it was clear that they were fed the same way. I suspect that the relatively haphazard way owners feed them (a little more one day and a little less the next) does not follow scientific method (*Resting energy requirement (18 lb dog) = 339 cal*), which causes the dogs' weight to fluctuate in a short period. Of course, many factors are affecting the weight of the hand, these are just my guesses based on the data. By the way, I envy her so much for having so many dogs, because I love dogs very much. My favourite dog is shiba inu!

<HR style="FILTER: alpha(opacity=100,finishopacity=0,style=1)" width="80%" color=#987cb9 SIZE=3>

<img src="image/god.gif" style="width:30%">
